mnemonics.title: Loving People Quickly Drives Happiness
mnemonics.content: LPQH = Linear Probing, Quadratic Probing, Double Hashing - the three main Open Addressing techniques that probe subsequent slots to resolve collisions

mnemonics.title: Separate Chains Hold Objects Neatly
mnemonics.content: SCHO = Separate Chaining - uses linked lists to handle multiple elements at the same hash index

mnemonics.title: Open Addressing Offers Options
mnemonics.content: OAOO = Open Addressing finds another open slot within the same table rather than using external structures

mnemonics.title: Linear Probes Step by Step
mnemonics.content: Linear Probing checks i+1, i+2, i+3... sequentially until empty slot found (simple: step = 1)

mnemonics.title: Quadratic Probing Jumps and Grows
mnemonics.content: Quadratic Probing uses i+1², i+2², i+3²... with step size increasing quadratically to reduce primary clustering

mnemonics.title: Double Hashing Uses Two Functions
mnemonics.content: Double Hashing: slot = (h1(key) + i × h2(key)) mod tableSize - uses secondary hash function to calculate step size

acronyms.usage: When you need simple implementation and good cache performance; avoid when table load factor > 0.5
When you want to reduce primary clustering seen in linear probing; check table size is prime for full coverage
When you need best distribution and minimal clustering; use when table size and h2(key) are coprime
When table load factor may exceed 0.5 or when you need predictable O(1+α) performance; uses linked lists
When you want memory efficiency and good cache performance; works best with load factor < 0.5
Ratio of elements (n) to table slots (m); determines when to rehash and affects performance

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hallway of lockers. If your locker is taken, you check the next one, then the next, until you find an empty spot - like finding a seat in a nearly full movie theater by walking down the row
Think of hopping on stones in a pond: first jump 1 step, then 4 steps (2²), then 9 steps (3²), then 16 steps - the gap between jumps keeps growing
Two dice rolled together - one decides starting position, the other decides step size. Each key gets a unique combination, like two different keys for two different locks
A school locker with a hook for multiple coats. Each hash index is a hook that can hold multiple elements hanging on a chain (linked list)
Imagine a traffic jam forming behind one blocked road - more cars pile up at the same spot, making the jam worse. This happens when many keys cluster around the same empty slot in linear probing
Like cars taking the same alternative routes even from different origins - keys with different starting points but same step size follow the same probe sequence (issue in quadratic probing)

analogies.analogy: A librarian assigning book codes. The floor number (hash function) decides which shelf a book goes on, but two different books might need the same shelf - that's a collision
Parking in a crowded mall. If your assigned spot is taken, you drive to the next spot, then next, until you find an empty parking space
Checking seats in an auditorium: first check seat 1 away, then 4 seats away (2²), then 9 seats away (3²) - jumping farther each time
Two security guards at different checkpoints. One decides where to start searching, the other decides how many steps to skip - together they create unique search patterns for each person
Each floor in a parking garage has multiple levels (like a linked list). If floor 3 is full, you park on the next available level on that same floor
Occupancy rate of a hotel. At 90% occupancy, finding a room becomes difficult and time-consuming. Hash tables perform best below 50% occupancy
Moving to a bigger house when your family grows. You get a new, larger table and redistribute all your furniture (old elements) into new positions
